Caption: Simulative behavior of the proposed aging model after 500 cycles of simulated aging with 0.5 C charging and discharging. Charging at 0.2 C (a)–(b), 0.5 C (c)–(d), and 0.75 C (e)–(f), neglecting particle crack propagation (blue), LAM (green), and SEI growth (orange) submodels. The magnification subplots demonstrate the consequences of excluding the respective submodels when predicting the anode potential. (b), (d) and (f) depict the absolute deviation from the reference simulation (red, dashed) in dotted lines.

MCC charging initiated at 1.5 C with various simulation configurations for impact analysis, neglecting the indicated aging submodels of the previously proposed aging-sensitive model. Simulation is conducted after 500 cycles of aging at predominantly 0.5 C charge/discharge. The current is recursively reduced by 1% upon reaching the 20 mV threshold, resulting in a steadily decreasing C-rate during MCC (a). Model-predicted negative electrode potential at the separator interface (b), model-predicted positive electrode potential at the separator interface (c), and model-predicted full-cell voltage (d).

Validation of the peak tracking approach by fitting the aged negative half-cell (left), positive half-cell (right), and full-cell (middle) charging curves over 500 cycles to the respective, initial charging curves after 50 cycles (a) – (c) . The accompanying fitted and measured DVA curves (d) – (f) .

Absolute DC-pulse resistance values obtained from 1 C DC pulses during RPTs over the course of the aging study for the negative half-cell (a), positive half-cell (b), and full-cell (c), evaluated over a 10 s timespan in the charging direction. The dominant increase in resistance from the positive half-cell is recognizable.
